lesson_title: Introduction to iostream: cout, cin, and endl

cards.front: What is the input/output library (io library)?
cards.back: The io library is part of the C++ standard library that deals with basic input and output. We use it to get input from the keyboard and output data to the console.
cards.source: 1.5 - Introduction to iostream: cout, cin, and endl

cards.text: To use std::cout, you must include the {{c1::<iostream>}} header.
cards.source: 1.5 - Introduction to iostream: cout, cin, and endl

cards.text: The {{c1::<<}} operator is called the {{c2::insertion}} operator when used with std::cout.
cards.source: 1.5 - Introduction to iostream: cout, cin, and endl

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: Multiple insertion operations can be chained together. The output is concatenated without any automatic separation.
cards.source: 1.5 - Introduction to iostream: cout, cin, and endl

cards.text: {{c1::std::endl}} outputs a newline character and {{c2::flushes}} the output buffer.
cards.source: 1.5 - Introduction to iostream: cout, cin, and endl

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: std::endl outputs a newline, moving the cursor to the next line. Each statement outputs text followed by a newline.
cards.source: 1.5 - Introduction to iostream: cout, cin, and endl

cards.front: What is the performance concern with std::endl?
cards.back: std::endl flushes the output buffer after outputting a newline, which can cause performance issues if used repeatedly. Use '\n' instead for better performance in most cases.
cards.source: 1.5 - Introduction to iostream: cout, cin, and endl

cards.text: The {{c1::>>}} operator is called the {{c2::extraction}} operator when used with std::cin.
cards.source: 1.5 - Introduction to iostream: cout, cin, and endl

cards.question: What does this code do?
cards.answer: Reads an integer from the keyboard and stores it in variable x
cards.explanation: std::cin with the extraction operator >> reads input from the keyboard. The program waits for user input before continuing.
cards.source: 1.5 - Introduction to iostream: cout, cin, and endl

cards.text: You can input multiple values in a single statement: {{c1::std::cin >> x >> y;}}
cards.source: 1.5 - Introduction to iostream: cout, cin, and endl

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Using wrong operator: should be << (insertion) not >> (extraction)
cards.explanation: Use << with std::cout for output and >> with std::cin for input. Using the wrong operator will cause a compile error.
cards.source: 1.5 - Introduction to iostream: cout, cin, and endl

cards.explanation:
Use \n for newlines in most cases for better performance. Only use std::endl when you need to:
  • Flush the output immediately
  • Ensure output appears before program waits for input
  • Debug timing-sensitive code
examples: std::cout << "Hello" << std::endl; // Use when flushing needed
std::cout << "Enter value: "; std::cin >> x; // endl not needed before cin
cards.source: 1.5 - Introduction to iostream: cout, cin, and endl
